original_abstract:
Distributional semantic models (DSMs) traditionally approximate word meaning by analyzing patterns of co-occurrence within large text corpora. While these models have proven effective for various computational linguistics tasks, they rely exclusively on symbolic linguistic data, ignoring the rich perceptual information that grounds human semantic knowledge. To bridge this gap, we propose a multimodal distributional framework that incorporates visual information into word representations.

By leveraging computer vision techniques, specifically the "bag-of-visual-words" approach, we map image features into discrete visual units that can be treated as contextual collocates. This allows us to extend the distributional hypothesis to encompass visual environments. We present a flexible architecture that integrates textual and visual features through Latent Multimodal Mixing and provides both Feature Level and Scoring Level fusion strategies. 

Empirical evaluations across semantic relatedness and concept categorization tasks demonstrate that our multimodal models consistently outperform purely text-based systems. Qualitative analysis reveals that visual features provide complementary information—particularly regarding concrete concepts and perceptual attributes—that is often missing or underrepresented in text. Our findings suggest that grounding distributional models in visual context significantly enhances the psychological plausibility and performance of computational semantic representations.
humanized_abstract:
Current state-of-the-art approaches to modeling word meaning in distributional semantic models (DSMs) rely on lexical co-occurrence patterns extracted from large datasets of text. As the representations are purely based on symbolic linguistic data, they fail to utilize the plethora of perceptual data associated with each word, which forms the cornerstone of human semantic grounding. In this paper, we aim at bridging this fundamental gap in current DSMs by introducing a multimodal distributional framework that seamlessly merges visual data with lexical information in order to produce more semantically robust representations.

Using computer vision and the “bag-of-visual-words” technique, the image features are converted to discrete visual units that can be treated as contextual collocates and hence, the distributional hypothesis is extended to the visual context. We propose a very flexible system which integrates the textual and visual features by means of Latent Multimodal Mixing and using Feature Level and Scoring Level fusion.

Experiments across semantic relatedness and concept categorization tasks demonstrate that the multimodal models presented in this paper yield improved performance over purely text-based approaches. Qualitative analysis into the behavior of the visual features shows that they can provide information that is not present or otherwise neglected in the text, such as for concrete objects and perceptual features. Overall, our results support the psychological validity of our multimodal distributional approach to semantic representation, by grounding distributional models into visual contexts that are more in-line with human understanding of meaning.
